question:
Suppose that $a_0 = 1$ and that $a_{n+1} = a_n + e^{-a_n}$ for $n=0,1,2,\dots$. Does $a_n - \log n$
have a finite limit as $n \to \infty$? (Here $\log n = \log_e n = \ln n$.)
solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
We will show that the answer is yes. First note that for all $x>-1$,
$e^x \geq 1+x$ and thus
\begin{equation} \label{eq:log}
x \geq \log(1+x).
\end{equation}
We next claim that $a_n > \log(n+1)$ (and in particular that $a_n-\log
n > 0$) for all $n$, by induction on $n$. For $n=0$ this follows
from $a_0=1$. Now suppose that $a_n > \log(n+1)$, and define $f(x)
= x+e^{-x}$, which is an increasing function in $x>0$; then
\begin{align*}
a_{n+1} &= f(a_n) > f(\log(n+1)) \\
&= \log(n+1) + 1/(n+1) \geq \log(n+2),
\end{align*}
where the last inequality is \eqref{eq:log} with $x=1/(n+1)$. This completes
the induction step.

It follows that $a_n-\log n$ is a decreasing function in $n$: we have
\begin{align*}
&(a_{n+1} - \log(n+1)) - (a_n - \log n) \\
&\,= e^{-a_n} + \log(n/(n+1)) \\
&\,< 1/(n+1) + \log(n/(n+1)) \leq 0,
\end{align*}
where the final inequality is \eqref{eq:log} with $x = -1/(n+1)$. Thus
$\{a_n-\log n\}_{n=0}^\infty$ is a decreasing sequence of positive numbers,
and so it has a limit as $n\to\infty$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
Put $b_n = e^{a_n}$, so that $b_{n+1} = b_n e^{1/b_n}$.  In terms of the
$b_n$, the problem is to prove that $b_n/n$ has a limit as $n \to \infty$;
we will show that the limit is in fact equal to 1.

Expanding $e^{1/b_n}$ as a Taylor series in $1/b_n$, we have
\[
b_{n+1} = b_n + 1 + R_n
\]
where $0 \leq R_n \leq c/b_n$ for some absolute constant $c>0$.
By writing
\[
b_n = n+e + \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} R_i,
\]
we see first that $b_n \geq n+e$. We then see that
\begin{align*}
0 &\leq \frac{b_n}{n} - 1 \\
&\leq \frac{e}{n} + \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{R_i}{n}  \\
&\leq \frac{e}{n} + \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{c}{n b_i}  \\
&\leq \frac{e}{n} + \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} \frac{c}{n (i+e)}  \\
&\leq \frac{e}{n} + \frac{c \log n}{n}.
\end{align*}
It follows that $b_n/n \to 1$ as $n \to \infty$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
This problem is an example of the general principle that one can often predict the asymptotic behavior of a recursive
sequence by studying solutions of a sufficiently similar-looking differential equation. In this case, we
start with the equation $a_{n+1} - a_n = e^{-a_n}$, then replace $a_n$ with a function $y(x)$
and replace the difference $a_{n+1} - a_n$ with the derivative $y'(x)$ to obtain the differential equation
$y' = e^{-y}$, which indeed has the solution $y = \log x$.

descriptive_long.question: Suppose that $initialvalue = 1$ and that $subsequent = sequenceterm + e^{-sequenceterm}$ for $indexer = 0,1,2,\dots$. Does $sequenceterm - \log indexer$ have a finite limit as $indexer \to \infty$? (Here $\log indexer = \log_e indexer = \ln indexer$.)
descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
We will show that the answer is yes. First note that for all $variablex>-1$, $e^{variablex} \geq 1+variablex$ and thus
\begin{equation} \label{eq:log}
variablex \geq \log(1+variablex).
\end{equation}
We next claim that $sequenceterm > \log(indexer+1)$ (and in particular that $sequenceterm-\log indexer > 0$) for all $indexer$, by induction on $indexer$. For $indexer=0$ this follows from $initialvalue=1$. Now suppose that $sequenceterm > \log(indexer+1)$, and define $transform(variablex)= variablex+e^{-variablex}$, which is an increasing function in $variablex>0$; then
\begin{align*}
subsequent &= transform(sequenceterm) > transform(\log(indexer+1)) \\
&= \log(indexer+1) + 1/(indexer+1) \geq \log(indexer+2),
\end{align*}
where the last inequality is \eqref{eq:log} with $variablex=1/(indexer+1)$. This completes the induction step.

It follows that $sequenceterm-\log indexer$ is a decreasing function in $indexer$: we have
\begin{align*}
&(subsequent - \log(indexer+1)) - (sequenceterm - \log indexer) \\
&\,= e^{-sequenceterm} + \log(indexer/(indexer+1)) \\
&\,< 1/(indexer+1) + \log(indexer/(indexer+1)) \leq 0,
\end{align*}
where the final inequality is \eqref{eq:log} with $variablex = -1/(indexer+1)$. Thus $\{sequenceterm-\log indexer\}_{indexer=0}^\infty$ is a decreasing sequence of positive numbers, and so it has a limit as $indexer\to\infty$.

\textbf{Second solution.}
Put $sequenceb = e^{sequenceterm}$, so that $subsequentb = sequenceb e^{1/sequenceb}$. In terms of the $sequenceb$, the problem is to prove that $sequenceb/indexer$ has a limit as $indexer \to \infty$; we will show that the limit is in fact equal to 1.

Expanding $e^{1/sequenceb}$ as a Taylor series in $1/sequenceb$, we have
\[
subsequentb = sequenceb + 1 + remainderterm
\]
where $0 \leq remainderterm \leq absoluteconst/sequenceb$ for some absolute constant $absoluteconst>0$. By writing
\[
sequenceb = indexer+e + \sum_{i=0}^{indexer-1} remainderterm,
\]
we see first that $sequenceb \geq indexer+e$. We then see that
\begin{align*}
0 &\leq \frac{sequenceb}{indexer} - 1 \\
&\leq \frac{e}{indexer} + \sum_{i=0}^{indexer-1} \frac{remainderterm}{indexer}  \\
&\leq \frac{e}{indexer} + \sum_{i=0}^{indexer-1} \frac{absoluteconst}{indexer\, sequenceb}  \\
&\leq \frac{e}{indexer} + \sum_{i=0}^{indexer-1} \frac{absoluteconst}{indexer (i+e)}  \\
&\leq \frac{e}{indexer} + \frac{absoluteconst \log indexer}{indexer}.
\end{align*}
It follows that $sequenceb/indexer \to 1$ as $indexer \to \infty$.

\textbf{Remark.}
This problem is an example of the general principle that one can often predict the asymptotic behavior of a recursive sequence by studying solutions of a sufficiently similar-looking differential equation. In this case, we start with the equation $subsequent - sequenceterm = e^{-sequenceterm}$, then replace $sequenceterm$ with a function $variabley(variablex)$ and replace the difference $subsequent - sequenceterm$ with the derivative $variabley'(variablex)$ to obtain the differential equation $variabley' = e^{-variabley}$, which indeed has the solution $variabley = \log variablex$.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Suppose that $peppermint = 1$ and that $marigolds = cinnamon + e^{-cinnamon}$ for $cardamom=0,1,2,\dots$. Does $cinnamon - \log cardamom$
have a finite limit as $cardamom \to \infty$? (Here $\log cardamom = \log_e cardamom = \ln cardamom$.)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
We will show that the answer is yes. First note that for all $safflower>-1$,
$e^{safflower} \geq 1+safflower$ and thus
\begin{equation} \label{eq:log}
safflower \geq \log(1+safflower).
\end{equation}
We next claim that $cinnamon > \log(cardamom+1)$ (and in particular that $cinnamon-\log
cardamom > 0$) for all $cardamom$, by induction on $cardamom$. For $cardamom=0$ this follows
from $peppermint=1$. Now suppose that $cinnamon > \log(cardamom+1)$, and define $grapeseed(safflower)
= safflower+e^{-safflower}$, which is an increasing function in $safflower>0$; then
\begin{align*}
marigolds &= grapeseed(cinnamon) > grapeseed(\log(cardamom+1)) \\
&= \log(cardamom+1) + 1/(cardamom+1) \geq \log(cardamom+2),
\end{align*}
where the last inequality is \eqref{eq:log} with $safflower=1/(cardamom+1)$. This completes
the induction step.

It follows that $cinnamon-\log cardamom$ is a decreasing function in $cardamom$: we have
\begin{align*}
&(marigolds - \log(cardamom+1)) - (cinnamon - \log cardamom) \\
&\,= e^{-cinnamon} + \log(cardamom/(cardamom+1)) \\
&\,< 1/(cardamom+1) + \log(cardamom/(cardamom+1)) \leq 0,
\end{align*}
where the final inequality is \eqref{eq:log} with $safflower = -1/(cardamom+1)$. Thus
$\{cinnamon-\log cardamom\}_{cardamom=0}^\infty$ is a decreasing sequence of positive numbers,
and so it has a limit as $cardamom\to\infty$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
Put $gingerale = e^{cinnamon}$, so that $butternut = gingerale e^{1/gingerale}$.  In terms of the
$gingerale$, the problem is to prove that $gingerale/cardamom$ has a limit as $cardamom \to \infty$;
we will show that the limit is in fact equal to 1.

Expanding $e^{1/gingerale}$ as a Taylor series in $1/gingerale$, we have
\[
butternut = gingerale + 1 + hazelnuts
\]
where $0 \leq hazelnuts \leq turmeric/gingerale$ for some absolute constant $turmeric>0$.
By writing
\[
gingerale = cardamom+e + \sum_{paprikas=0}^{cardamom-1} hazelnuts,
\]
we see first that $gingerale \geq cardamom+e$. We then see that
\begin{align*}
0 &\leq \frac{gingerale}{cardamom} - 1 \\
&\leq \frac{e}{cardamom} + \sum_{paprikas=0}^{cardamom-1} \frac{hazelnuts}{cardamom}  \\
&\leq \frac{e}{cardamom} + \sum_{paprikas=0}^{cardamom-1} \frac{turmeric}{cardamom \, gingerale}  \\
&\leq \frac{e}{cardamom} + \sum_{paprikas=0}^{cardamom-1} \frac{turmeric}{cardamom (paprikas+e)}  \\
&\leq \frac{e}{cardamom} + \frac{turmeric \log cardamom}{cardamom}.
\end{align*}
It follows that $gingerale/cardamom \to 1$ as $cardamom \to \infty$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
This problem is an example of the general principle that one can often predict the asymptotic behavior of a recursive
sequence by studying solutions of a sufficiently similar-looking differential equation. In this case, we
start with the equation $marigolds - cinnamon = e^{-cinnamon}$, then replace $cinnamon$ with a function $eldertree(safflower)$
and replace the difference $marigolds - cinnamon$ with the derivative $eldertree'(safflower)$ to obtain the differential equation
$eldertree' = e^{-eldertree}$, which indeed has the solution $eldertree = \log safflower$.

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Suppose that $infinitefinal = 1$ and that $previousterm = nextvalue + e^{-nextvalue}$ for $constantindex=0,1,2,\dots$. Does $nextvalue - \log constantindex$
have a finite limit as $constantindex \to \infty$? (Here $\log constantindex = \log_e constantindex = \ln constantindex$.)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
We will show that the answer is yes. First note that for all $outputvar>-1$,
$e^{outputvar} \geq 1+outputvar$ and thus
\begin{equation} \label{eq:log}
outputvar \geq \log(1+outputvar).
\end{equation}
We next claim that $nextvalue > \log(constantindex+1)$ (and in particular that $nextvalue-\log
constantindex > 0$) for all $constantindex$, by induction on $constantindex$. For $constantindex=0$ this follows
from $infinitefinal=1$. Now suppose that $nextvalue > \log(constantindex+1)$, and define $constantfun(outputvar)
= outputvar+e^{-outputvar}$, which is an increasing function in $outputvar>0$; then
\begin{align*}
previousterm &= constantfun(nextvalue) > constantfun(\log(constantindex+1)) \\
&= \log(constantindex+1) + 1/(constantindex+1) \geq \log(constantindex+2),
\end{align*}
where the last inequality is \eqref{eq:log} with $outputvar=1/(constantindex+1)$. This completes
the induction step.

It follows that $nextvalue-\log constantindex$ is a decreasing function in $constantindex$: we have
\begin{align*}
&(previousterm - \log(constantindex+1)) - (nextvalue - \log constantindex) \\
&\,= e^{-nextvalue} + \log(constantindex/(constantindex+1)) \\
&\,< 1/(constantindex+1) + \log(constantindex/(constantindex+1)) \leq 0,
\end{align*}
where the final inequality is \eqref{eq:log} with $outputvar = -1/(constantindex+1)$. Thus
$\{nextvalue-\log constantindex\}_{constantindex=0}^\infty$ is a decreasing sequence of positive numbers,
and so it has a limit as $constantindex\to\infty$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
Put $aftervalue = e^{nextvalue}$, so that $beforevalue = aftervalue e^{1/aftervalue}$.  In terms of the
$aftervalue$, the problem is to prove that $aftervalue/constantindex$ has a limit as $constantindex \to \infty$;
we will show that the limit is in fact equal to 1.

Expanding $e^{1/aftervalue}$ as a Taylor series in $1/aftervalue$, we have
\[
beforevalue = aftervalue + 1 + mainpart
\]
where $0 \leq mainpart \leq variableconst/aftervalue$ for some absolute constant $variableconst>0$.
By writing
\[
aftervalue = constantindex+e + \sum_{staticindex=0}^{constantindex-1} mainparti,
\]
we see first that $aftervalue \geq constantindex+e$. We then see that
\begin{align*}
0 &\leq \frac{aftervalue}{constantindex} - 1 \\
&\leq \frac{e}{constantindex} + \sum_{staticindex=0}^{constantindex-1} \frac{mainparti}{constantindex}  \\
&\leq \frac{e}{constantindex} + \sum_{staticindex=0}^{constantindex-1} \frac{variableconst}{constantindex postindex}  \\
&\leq \frac{e}{constantindex} + \sum_{staticindex=0}^{constantindex-1} \frac{variableconst}{constantindex (staticindex+e)}  \\
&\leq \frac{e}{constantindex} + \frac{variableconst \log constantindex}{constantindex}.
\end{align*}
It follows that $aftervalue/constantindex \to 1$ as $constantindex \to \infty$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
This problem is an example of the general principle that one can often predict the asymptotic behavior of a recursive
sequence by studying solutions of a sufficiently similar-looking differential equation. In this case, we
start with the equation $previousterm - nextvalue = e^{-nextvalue}$, then replace $nextvalue$ with a function $horizontalaxis(outputvar)$
and replace the difference $previousterm - nextvalue$ with the derivative $horizontalaxis'(outputvar)$ to obtain the differential equation
$horizontalaxis' = e^{-horizontalaxis}$, which indeed has the solution $horizontalaxis = \log outputvar$.

garbled_string.question:
Suppose that $qzxwvtnp = 1$ and that $hjgrksla = mldpxqve + e^{-mldpxqve}$ for $zpkjaqmr=0,1,2,\dots$. Does $mldpxqve - \log zpkjaqmr$
have a finite limit as $zpkjaqmr \to \infty$? (Here $\log zpkjaqmr = \log_e zpkjaqmr = \ln zpkjaqmr$.)
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution.}
We will show that the answer is yes. First note that for all $blmkduhe>-1$,
$e^{blmkduhe} \geq 1+blmkduhe$ and thus
\begin{equation} \label{eq:log}
blmkduhe \geq \log(1+blmkduhe).
\end{equation}
We next claim that $mldpxqve > \log(zpkjaqmr+1)$ (and in particular that $mldpxqve-\log zpkjaqmr > 0$) for all $zpkjaqmr$, by induction on $zpkjaqmr$. For $zpkjaqmr=0$ this follows
from $qzxwvtnp=1$. Now suppose that $mldpxqve > \log(zpkjaqmr+1)$, and define $yphrksam(blmkduhe)
= blmkduhe+e^{-blmkduhe}$, which is an increasing function in $blmkduhe>0$; then
\begin{align*}
hjgrksla &= yphrksam(mldpxqve) > yphrksam(\log(zpkjaqmr+1)) \\
&= \log(zpkjaqmr+1) + 1/(zpkjaqmr+1) \geq \log(zpkjaqmr+2),
\end{align*}
where the last inequality is \eqref{eq:log} with $blmkduhe=1/(zpkjaqmr+1)$. This completes
the induction step.

It follows that $mldpxqve-\log zpkjaqmr$ is a decreasing function in $zpkjaqmr$: we have
\begin{align*}
&(hjgrksla - \log(zpkjaqmr+1)) - (mldpxqve - \log zpkjaqmr) \\
&\,= e^{-mldpxqve} + \log(zpkjaqmr/(zpkjaqmr+1)) \\
&\,< 1/(zpkjaqmr+1) + \log(zpkjaqmr/(zpkjaqmr+1)) \leq 0,
\end{align*}
where the final inequality is \eqref{eq:log} with $blmkduhe = -1/(zpkjaqmr+1)$. Thus
$\{mldpxqve-\log zpkjaqmr\}_{zpkjaqmr=0}^\infty$ is a decreasing sequence of positive numbers,
and so it has a limit as $zpkjaqmr\to\infty$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution.}
Put $kfmdsajo = e^{mldpxqve}$, so that $tcvwralh = kfmdsajo e^{1/kfmdsajo}$.  In terms of the
$kfmdsajo$, the problem is to prove that $kfmdsajo/zpkjaqmr$ has a limit as $zpkjaqmr \to \infty$;
we will show that the limit is in fact equal to 1.

Expanding $e^{1/kfmdsajo}$ as a Taylor series in $1/kfmdsajo$, we have
\[
tcvwralh = kfmdsajo + 1 + jsvhtpoc
\]
where $0 \leq jsvhtpoc \leq vzrdlkho/kfmdsajo$ for some absolute constant $vzrdlkho>0$.
By writing
\[
kfmdsajo = zpkjaqmr+e + \sum_{lodqweuv=0}^{zpkjaqmr-1} R_{lodqweuv},
\]
we see first that $kfmdsajo \geq zpkjaqmr+e$. We then see that
\begin{align*}
0 &\leq \frac{kfmdsajo}{zpkjaqmr} - 1 \\
&\leq \frac{e}{zpkjaqmr} + \sum_{lodqweuv=0}^{zpkjaqmr-1} \frac{R_{lodqweuv}}{zpkjaqmr}  \\
&\leq \frac{e}{zpkjaqmr} + \sum_{lodqweuv=0}^{zpkjaqmr-1} \frac{vzrdlkho}{zpkjaqmr rgnvtecu}  \\
&\leq \frac{e}{zpkjaqmr} + \sum_{lodqweuv=0}^{zpkjaqmr-1} \frac{vzrdlkho}{zpkjaqmr (lodqweuv+e)}  \\
&\leq \frac{e}{zpkjaqmr} + \frac{vzrdlkho \log zpkjaqmr}{zpkjaqmr}.
\end{align*}
It follows that $kfmdsajo/zpkjaqmr \to 1$ as $zpkjaqmr \to \infty$.

\noindent
\textbf{Remark.}
This problem is an example of the general principle that one can often predict the asymptotic behavior of a recursive
sequence by studying solutions of a sufficiently similar-looking differential equation. In this case, we
start with the equation $hjgrksla - mldpxqve = e^{-mldpxqve}$, then replace $mldpxqve$ with a function $wgfnspie(blmkduhe)$
and replace the difference $hjgrksla - mldpxqve$ with the derivative $wgfnspie'(blmkduhe)$ to obtain the differential equation
$wgfnspie' = e^{-wgfnspie}$, which indeed has the solution $wgfnspie = \log blmkduhe$.

kernel_variant.question:
Let $b_0 = 2$ and define 
\[
 b_{n+1}= b_n + e^{-b_n}\qquad(n=0,1,2,\dots).
\]
Determine whether the sequence $\bigl\{\,b_n-\ln(n+2)\,\bigr\}_{n\ge 0}$ converges as $n\to\infty$, and justify your answer.
kernel_variant.solution:
We claim that

  b_n - ln(n+2) \to  0    as n\to \infty ,

so in particular the sequence converges to the finite limit 0.

Proof.  Define

  B_n = e^{b_n}.

Then from b_{n+1} = b_n + e^{-b_n} we get

  B_{n+1} = e^{b_{n+1}} = e^{b_n}\cdot e^{e^{-b_n}} = B_n\cdot e^{1/B_n}.

We now expand the exponential in a one-term Taylor remainder form.  For t>0,

  e^t = 1 + t + R(t),    where   R(t) = e^\xi \cdot t^2/2   for some \xi \in [0,t].

Apply this with t=1/B_n.  Then

  e^{1/B_n} = 1 + 1/B_n + R_n,

where R_n = O(1/B_n^2); in particular there is a constant C such that

  0 \leq  R_n \leq  C/B_n^2.

Hence

  B_{n+1} = B_n\cdot (1 + 1/B_n + R_n) = B_n + 1 + (R_n\cdot B_n)
          = B_n + 1 + E_n,

where

  0 \leq  E_n = R_n\cdot B_n \leq  C/B_n \leq  C/(n+2)

(since trivially B_n = e^{b_n} \geq  e^{ln(n+2)} = n+2 by the easy lower-bound b_n>ln(n+2)).

Summing this recursion from k=0 to k=n-1 gives

  B_n = B_0 + n + \sum _{k=0}^{n-1} E_k = e^2 + n + \sum _{k=0}^{n-1}E_k.

But 0 \leq  \sum _{k=0}^{n-1}E_k \leq  C\sum _{k=0}^{n-1}1/(k+2) = O(log n).  Therefore

  B_n = n + O(log n)

and more precisely

  B_n/(n+2) = 1 + O((log n)/(n+2)) \to  1.

Taking logarithms,

  b_n = ln B_n = ln(n+2) + ln(B_n/(n+2))

and since B_n/(n+2)\to 1, ln(B_n/(n+2))\to 0.  Hence

  b_n - ln(n+2) \to  0

as claimed.  In particular, the sequence {b_n - ln(n+2)} converges (to 0).

core_steps: Apply e^x ≥ 1 + x  (⇔  x ≥ log(1+x)) to relate linear and logarithmic terms.
Use monotonicity of f(x)=x+e^{-x} plus induction to prove a_n > log(n+1).
Compute Δ_n = (a_{n+1}-log(n+1))-(a_n-log n) and show Δ_n ≤ 0 with the same inequality, hence {a_n−log n} is decreasing.
Observe that a_n−log n ≥ 0 from Step 2, so the sequence is bounded below.
Invoke the monotone-convergence principle: a positive, decreasing sequence has a finite limit.

slot1.description: Initial value of the recursion; any choice that already exceeds log 1 keeps the induction base valid.

slot2.description: Constant multiplier in the update rule (a_{n+1}=a_n + c·e^{−a_n}); any c ≥ 1 still makes k/(n+1) dominate log(1+1/(n+1)).

slot3.description: Fixed shift in the comparison term log(n+β); any β > 0 works because log(n+β+1)−log(n+β) ≤ 1/(n+β).
